narrative_blocks.header: Rector of Emmanuel Episcopal Church, Pittsburgh
narrative_blocks.body: George Richards served about ten years as rector of Emmanuel Protestant Episcopal Church in Pittsburgh. He and his wife later settled at Perry, New York, where both are buried.

narrative_blocks.header: Rector of Emmanuel Episcopal Church, Pittsburgh
narrative_blocks.body: George Richards served about ten years as rector of Emmanuel Protestant Episcopal Church in Pittsburgh. He and his wife later settled at Perry, New York, where both are buried.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale founder; hosted the Saybrook Platform 1708

narrative_blocks.header: Hosted the Saybrook Platform in his own house
narrative_blocks.body: On September 9, 1708, the Connecticut Synod assembled at his Saybrook home: twelve ministers, four laymen, eight of the twelve being Yale Trustees. The result was the Saybrook Platform — the document that defined Connecticut Congregationalism for the next century, a carefully negotiated middle way between Massachusetts and Presbyterian practice.

narrative_blocks.header: One of twelve original Trustees of Yale College
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the twelve ministers who signed Yale's founding documents in 1701. He died April 1, 1709 — "second in age among the Trustees, and for forty-three years pastor of Saybrook." His son Stephen received Yale's first academic degree at its first commencement.

narrative_blocks.header: His son had married his new wife's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Buckingham married the widow Mary Willet Hooker in 1703, his son Stephen was already the husband of her daughter Sarah. The father and son had married a mother and her daughter.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Cemetery, Old Saybrook, Middlesex County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Founding trustee of Yale; minister at Saybrook

career.notes: Hosted the Saybrook Synod of 1708 in his own house; a founding trustee of the Collegiate School that became Yale College.

parents.father_research_notes: X01001 married I00006 Mary Willet (widow of H00007 Samuel Hooker) in 1703. So Stephen's father became the stepfather of Stephen's wife Sarah's mother.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery, Norwalk, Fairfield County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: First name in Yale's first Triennial Catalogue
narrative_blocks.body: At Yale's first commencement in 1702, Stephen received his second academic degree — the first awarded by the new Collegiate School. His name appears first in the Triennial Catalogue of Yale College, making him the college's first officially recorded graduate, despite having taken his first degree at Harvard in 1693.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left everything to his wife Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen's will, dated January 1, 1745/46 and proved February 18 in Fairfield, was simple and complete: he left everything to his wife Sarah. They had been married nearly sixty years.

Jedidiah Buckingham — Stephen's nephew — visited him in Norwalk in 1720. He had recently left his pulpit in Newark, New Jersey. He died at Stephen's house March 28, 1720, aged 24, and was buried in Norwalk.

narrative_blocks.header: Cleared of charges — then pushed out anyway
narrative_blocks.body: After thirty years at Norwalk, Buckingham was charged with intemperance. A Consociation investigated and found no fault in his conduct. The town then found a letter they could construe as a resignation and accepted it before he could object.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's mother married his own father
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Buckingham married Sarah Hooker of Farmington. Her widowed mother, Mary Willet, later married his father, Rev. Thomas Buckingham, making his mother-in-law his stepmother as well.

cross_connections.display_label: his brother-in-law, who likewise married a Hooker and helped found Yale

education.notes: Received his second degree at Yale's first commencement; his name stands first in the college's Triennial Catalogue. Later a member of the Yale Corporation.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Norwalk pastor; among Yale’s first graduates

career.notes: Ordained Nov 17, 1697; served about thirty years; resigned Feb 24, 1727.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery; no stone marks the grave (tradition). SOURCE: EH/FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: The most accomplished lady Norwalk had ever seen
narrative_blocks.body: She was nicknamed 'Dame Buckingham' and described in family tradition as 'the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.' She lived at Norwalk with her husband Rev. Stephen Buckingham from their 1703 marriage until her death in 1759.

narrative_blocks.header: No stone marks her grave — tradition knows
narrative_blocks.body: She died about March 26, 1759 in Norwalk, age 77. No marker survives at her grave; the location is preserved only in family tradition and her husband's burial records.

narrative_blocks.header: Her stepfather's son became her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After her father Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, her mother Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook. Thomas Buckingham was the father of Rev. Stephen Buckingham — whom Sarah herself married. After Thomas died in 1709, Mary came to Sarah and Stephen at Norwalk and lived with them until her death in 1712.

bio.bio_blurb: "Dame Buckingham" of Norwalk; granddaughter of Thomas Hooker

burialCemetery.name: Glenwood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Glenwood Cemetery, Perry, Wyoming County, New York.
